q: The minimum distance of a point on the curve <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></math> from the origin is

solution:
A variable point on the given parabola can be taken as α, α2-4



Distance from origin D= α2+α2-42

⇒ D2=α2+α4+16-8α2

=α4-7α2+16

dD2dα=4α3-14 α=0

⇒ 2α2α2-7=0

⇒ α=0, 72, -72



∴ Dmin 2 at α2=72

Dmin2=494-492+16

= -494+16=154

Dmin=152

question: If <math><mi>θ</mi></math> denotes the acute angle between the curves, <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> and <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> at a point of their intersection, then <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">tan</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mrow></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:

∴m1=4 or -4

For 2nd curve, dydx=2x

∴m2=-4 or 4

∴ |tanθ|=4--41+4-4 or -4-41+-44

=815 (In both cases)

subject: Mathematics
q: A spherical balloon is being inflated at the rate of $35 \mathrm{cc} / \mathrm{min}$. The rate of increase in the surface area (in $\mathrm{cm}^2 / \mathrm{min}$.) of the balloon when its diameter is $14 \mathrm{~cm}$, is :
question: A spherical balloon is being inflated at the rate of $35 \mathrm{cc} / \mathrm{min}$. The rate of increase in the surface area (in $\mathrm{cm}^2 / \mathrm{min}$.) of the balloon when its diameter is $14 \mathrm{~cm}$, is :
solution:
Volume of sphere $\mathrm{V}=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$
$\frac{d \mathrm{~V}}{d t}=\frac{4}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot 3 r^2 \cdot \frac{d r}{d t}$
$35=4 \pi r^2 \cdot \frac{d r}{d t}$ or $\frac{d r}{d t}=\frac{35}{4 \pi r^2}$
Surface area of sphere $=\mathrm{S}=4 \pi r^2$
$\frac{d \mathrm{~S}}{d t}=4 \pi \times 2 r \times \frac{d r}{d t}=8 \pi r \cdot \frac{d r}{d t}$
$\frac{d \mathrm{~S}}{d t}=\frac{70}{r}$
Now, diameter $=14 \mathrm{~cm}, r=7$
$\therefore \quad \frac{d \mathrm{~S}}{d t}=10$

subject: Mathematics
q: The normal to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The normal to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
options: Meets the curve again in the fourth quadrant
Does not meet the curve again
Meets the curve again in the second quadrant
Meets the curve again in the third quadrant
solution:
Given x2+2xy-3y2=0

x+3yx-y=0

Pair of straight lines passing through the origin.

∵ x+3y=0  or  x-y=0

Normal exists at 1, 1 which is on x-y=0

⇒   The slope of normal at 1, 1=-1 

∴   Equation of normal will be

y-1=-x-1    y-y1=mx-x1

y-1=-x+1

x+y=2

Now, find the point of intersection with x+3y=0. 

   x+y=2x+3y=0
  ----------------
   -2y=2   ⇒y=-1, x=3

∵ 3, -1 lies in the fourth quadrant.

q: If the curves, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>16</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> touch each other at a point, then the largest value of <math><mi>k</mi></math> is ____________.
question: If the curves, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>16</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi></mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> touch each other at a point, then the largest value of <math><mi>k</mi></math> is ____________.
solution:
If general equation of circle is x2+y2+2gx+2fy+c=0 then its center is -g,-f and radius is g2+f2-c

Two circles touches each other if C1C2=r1±r2

Distance between C23,0andC10,4 is either  k+1ork-1

C1C2=3-02+0-42=5

⇒k+1=5or k+1=5  

⇒k=16 or k=36

⇒ Maximum value of k is 36.

q: A box open from top is made from a rectangular sheet of dimension a×b by cutting squares each of side x from each of the four corners and folding up the flaps. If the volume of the box is maximum, then x is equal to:
question: A box open from top is made from a rectangular sheet of dimension a×b by cutting squares each of side x from each of the four corners and folding up the flaps. If the volume of the box is maximum, then x is equal to:
solution:
Given that:  A box open from top is made from a rectangular sheet dimension a×b by cutting squares each of side x from each of the four corners and folding up the flaps and the volume of the box is maximum.

dimensions of the box:

L=a-2x, B=b-2x & H=x

V=L×B×H

V(x)=(a-2 x)(b-2 x) x

V(x)=4x3-2(a+b)x2+abx

For volume to be maximum:
V'(x)=12x2-4(a+b)x+ab=0

x=4(a+b)-16a2+16b2-16ab24+ cannot be taken as x<a,b,
for maximum volume, x=a+b-a2+b2-ab6

q: If <math><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is a non-zero polynomial of degree four, having local extreme points at <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn><mo>;</mo></math> then the set <math><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>}</mo></math> contains exactly
question: If <math><mi>f</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is a non-zero polynomial of degree four, having local extreme points at <math><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi> </mi><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>1</mn><mo>;</mo></math> then the set <math><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>{</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi><mi> </mi><mo>:</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>}</mo></math> contains exactly
options: Two irrational and two rational numbers
Two irrational and one rational number
solution:
f(x) has extreme points at {-1, 0, 1}

⇒f'x=0  at x= –1, 0, 1

⇒f'x=ax2-1x  ∵f x is a polynomial

⇒fx=∫ax3-xdx=ax44-x22+ C

Now the equation,

fx=f(0)

⇒ax44-x22+ C=C
⇒ x44-x22=0 ⇒x2x2-2=0
⇒x=0, ±2

⇒S= {0, 2, -2}.

Hence, the set contains two irrational and one rational number.

solution:
f'(x)=0 for maximum value
Let y=2xx2

lny=x2ln2x

1yy'=2xln2x+x212x×-2x2

y'=(xy)2ln2x-1

y'=2xx2x2ln2x-1

2ln2x=1

2x=e12

x=2e-12

Then maximum value will be
f2e-12=22e-124e-1=e2e-1=e2e

q:
Let a function f:0,5→R be continuous, f1=3 and F be defined as:
Fx=∫1xt2gtdt, where gt=∫1tfudu.

Then for the function Fx, the point x=1 is:
question:
Let a function f:0,5→R be continuous, f1=3 and F be defined as:
Fx=∫1xt2gtdt, where gt=∫1tfudu.

Then for the function Fx, the point x=1 is:
solution:
Given, Fx=∫1xt2gtdt

By Leibnitz rule we get, 

F'x=x2gx 

⇒F'1=1.g1=0 ∵g1=0

Now F''x=2xgx+x2g'x

⇒F''x=2xgx+x2fx ∵g'x=fx

⇒F''1=0+1×3

⇒F''1=3

Fx has a local minimum at x=1.

q: If the function $f(x)=2 x^3-9 \mathrm{ax}^2+12 \mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{x}+1$, where $\mathrm{a} \gt 0$, attains its local maximum and local minimum values at $p$ and $q$, respectively, such that $\mathrm{p}^2=\mathrm{q}$, then $f(3)$ is equal to:
question: If the function $f(x)=2 x^3-9 \mathrm{ax}^2+12 \mathrm{a}^2 \mathrm{x}+1$, where $\mathrm{a} \gt 0$, attains its local maximum and local minimum values at $p$ and $q$, respectively, such that $\mathrm{p}^2=\mathrm{q}$, then $f(3)$ is equal to:
solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=6 x^2-18 a x+12 a^2$
$f^{\prime}(x)=6\left(x^2-3 a x+2 a^2\right)$
roots are $a, 2 a$
$\mathrm{p}^2=\mathrm{q} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}^2=2 \mathrm{a}$
$a=2$
$f(x)=2 x^3-18 x^2+48 x+1$
$f(3)=37$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a twice differentiable function such that $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and $f'(a-1) = 0$, where $a$ is a real number. Let $g(x) = f\left( \tan^{2}x - 2\tan x + a \right), \; 0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2}$.

Consider the following two statements:
(I) $g$ is increasing in $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$
(II) $g$ is decreasing in $\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$. Then,
question:
Let $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a twice differentiable function such that $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ and $f'(a-1) = 0$, where $a$ is a real number. Let $g(x) = f\left( \tan^{2}x - 2\tan x + a \right), \; 0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2}$.

Consider the following two statements:
(I) $g$ is increasing in $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$
(II) $g$ is decreasing in $\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$. Then,
solution:
Given $f''(x) > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$, which means $f'(x)$ is a strictly increasing function.
We are given $f'(a-1) = 0$. Since $f'(x)$ is strictly increasing, $f'(x) < 0$ for $x < a-1$ and $f'(x) > 0$ for $x > a-1$.
The function $g(x)$ is defined as $g(x) = f(\tan^2 x - 2\tan x + a)$ for $x \in (0, \pi/2)$.
Let $u(x) = \tan^2 x - 2\tan x + a = (\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1$.
Differentiating $g(x)$ with respect to $x$:
$g'(x) = f'(u(x)) \cdot u'(x) = f'((\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1) \cdot (2\tan x \sec^2 x - 2\sec^2 x)$
$g'(x) = f'((\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1) \cdot 2\sec^2 x (\tan x - 1)$.

Case 1: $x \in (0, \pi/4)$.
In this interval, $0 < \tan x < 1$, so $(\tan x - 1) < 0$.
Also, $(\tan x - 1)^2 > 0$, so $u(x) = (\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1 > a - 1$.
Since $f'(x)$ is increasing and $f'(a-1) = 0$, for $u(x) > a - 1$, we have $f'(u(x)) > 0$.
Thus, $g'(x) = (+)(+)(-) < 0$. So $g(x)$ is decreasing in $(0, \pi/4)$.
Statement (I) is False.

Case 2: $x \in (\pi/4, \pi/2)$.
In this interval, $\tan x > 1$, so $(\tan x - 1) > 0$.
Also, $(\tan x - 1)^2 > 0$, so $u(x) = (\tan x - 1)^2 + a - 1 > a - 1$.
Thus, $f'(u(x)) > 0$.
Then $g'(x) = (+)(+)(+) > 0$. So $g(x)$ is increasing in $(\pi/4, \pi/2)$.
Statement (II) is False.

Therefore, neither (I) nor (II) is true.

q: The shortest distance between the line <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and the curve <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></math> is
question: The shortest distance between the line <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math> and the curve <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></math> is
solution:
y2=x–2
Differentiating w.r.t. x we get,
2yy'= 1⇒y'=  12y
For the shortest distance, the tangent at point P  will be parallel to the given line
⇒y'|p=12y1=1⇒y1=12
⇒x1=2+122=94 (∵y12=x1-2)
The shortest distance between the given curve & the line
= The perpendicular distance of point P from the line
=94-1212+12=742=742

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> be an integer such that all the real roots of the polynomial <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>5</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn></math> lie in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>|</mo></math> is equal to ______.
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> be an integer such that all the real roots of the polynomial <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>5</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn></math> lie in the interval <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>|</mo></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
Let 2x5+5x4+10x3+10x2+10x+10=fx

Now f-2=-34 and f-1=3

Hence fx has a root in -2,-1

Further f'x=10x4+20x3+30x2+20x+10

=10x2x2+2x+3+2x+1x2

=10x2x2+1x2+2x+1x+3

=10x2x+1x2-2+2x+1x+3

=10x2x+1x+12>0  for all x belongs to R.

fx is strictly increasing function. Since it is an odd degree polynomial it will have exactly one real root.

Hence, fx has only one real root, so a=2.

q: A square piece of tin of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is to be made into a box without top by cutting a square from each corner and folding up the flaps to form a box. If the volume of the box is maximum, then its surface area (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>) is equal to
question: A square piece of tin of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is to be made into a box without top by cutting a square from each corner and folding up the flaps to form a box. If the volume of the box is maximum, then its surface area (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>) is equal to
solution:
Given that, the side of square is 30 cm and x cm squares are cut off.

The required diagram is,

Now the dimensions of the cuboid formed will be 

lx=30-2x, bx=30-2x and hx=x.

The Volume of the cuboid will be Vx=30-2x2x

Now to get Maximum value,

⇒dVxdx=0

⇒230-2x-2x+30-2x21=0

⇒30-2x-4x+30-2x=0

On simplifying we get, 

⇒x=15 cm, 5 cm

But x cannot be 15 cm as the volume becomes zero.

Hence x=5 cm.

Now to find the surface area of the cuboid,

Surface area will be =30-2x×x×4+30-2x2

=30-2×5×5×4+30-2×52

=800 cm2.

Therefore, the required surface area will be 800 cm2.

subject: Mathematics
q: The function $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}(\sin x+\cos x)$ is an increasing function in
question: The function $f(x)=\tan ^{-1}(\sin x+\cos x)$ is an increasing function in
solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{1+(\sin x+\cos x)^2}(\cos x-\sin x)$
$=\frac{\sqrt{2} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{1+(\sin x+\cos x)^2}$
$f(x)$ is increasing if $-\frac{\pi}{2} < x+\frac{\pi}{4} < \frac{\pi}{2}$
$-\frac{3 \pi}{4} < x < \frac{\pi}{4}$
hence $f(x)$ is increasing when $x \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider a rectangle whose length is increasing at the uniform rate of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$, breadth is decreasing at the uniform rate of $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$ and the area is decreasing at the uniform rate of $5 \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{sec}$. If after some time the breadth of the rectangle is $2 \mathrm{~m}$ then the length of the rectangle is
question: Consider a rectangle whose length is increasing at the uniform rate of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$, breadth is decreasing at the uniform rate of $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$ and the area is decreasing at the uniform rate of $5 \mathrm{~m}^2 / \mathrm{sec}$. If after some time the breadth of the rectangle is $2 \mathrm{~m}$ then the length of the rectangle is
solution:
Let $A$ be the area, $b$ be the breadth and $\ell$ be the length of the rectangle.
Given: $\frac{d A}{d t}=-5, \frac{d \ell}{d t}=2, \frac{d b}{d t}=-3$
We know, $A=\ell \times b$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d t}=\ell \cdot \frac{d b}{d t}+b \cdot \frac{d \ell}{d t}=-3 \ell+2 b \\
& \Rightarrow-5=-3 \ell+2 b .
\end{aligned}
$$
When $b=2$, we have
$$
-5=-3 \ell+4 \Rightarrow \ell=\frac{9}{3}=3 m
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: $\max_{0 \leq x \leq \pi}\left(16\sin\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\cos^3\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\right)$ is equal to:
question: $\max_{0 \leq x \leq \pi}\left(16\sin\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\cos^3\left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)\right)$ is equal to:
solution:
Let $y = \dfrac{x}{2}$. Since $0 \leq x \leq \pi$, we have $0 \leq y \leq \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

The given expression becomes $f(y) = 16\sin y \cos^3 y$.

Differentiating with respect to $y$:

$f'(y) = 16(\cos y \cdot \cos^3 y + \sin y \cdot 3\cos^2 y(-\sin y))$

$f'(y) = 16\cos^2 y(\cos^2 y - 3\sin^2 y)$

For maximum value, $f'(y) = 0$:

$\cos^2 y - 3\sin^2 y = 0 \Rightarrow \tan^2 y = \dfrac{1}{3}$

Since $y \in \left[0, \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right]$, $\tan y = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$.

Substituting $y = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ in $f(y)$:

$f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = 16\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)\cos^3\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$

$f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = 16 \times \dfrac{1}{2} \times \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^3$

$f\left(\dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = 8 \times \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{8} = 3\sqrt{3}$

The maximum value is $3\sqrt{3}$.

Answer: $3\sqrt{3}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If a metallic circular plate of radius $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ is heated so that its radius increases at the rate of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ per hour, then the rate at which, the area of the plate increases (in $\mathrm{cm}^2 /$ hour) is
question: If a metallic circular plate of radius $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ is heated so that its radius increases at the rate of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ per hour, then the rate at which, the area of the plate increases (in $\mathrm{cm}^2 /$ hour) is
solution:
Let $A=\pi \mathrm{r}^2$ be area of metalic circular plate of $r=50 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Also, given $\frac{d r}{d t}=1 \mathrm{~mm}=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~cm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore A=\pi r^2 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d t}=2 \pi r \frac{d r}{d t}=2 \pi \cdot 50 \cdot \frac{1}{10}=10 \pi
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, area of plate increases in $10 \pi \mathrm{cm}^2 /$ hour.

q:
The maximum distance from origin of a point on the curve $x=a \sin t-b \sin \left(\frac{a t}{b}\right)$ 
$y=a \cos t-b \cos \left(\frac{a t}{b}\right)$, both $a, b>0$ is
question:
The maximum distance from origin of a point on the curve $x=a \sin t-b \sin \left(\frac{a t}{b}\right)$ 
$y=a \cos t-b \cos \left(\frac{a t}{b}\right)$, both $a, b>0$ is
solution:
Distance of origin from $(x, y)=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}$
$$
=\sqrt{a^2+b^2-2 a b \cos \left(t-\frac{a t}{b}\right)}=\sqrt{a^2+b^2-2 a b}\left[\because \max \cdot \cos \left(t-\frac{a t}{b}\right)=1\right]=a-b
$$

question: The sum of all the local minimum values of the twice differentiable function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>"</mo></msup><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>"</mo></msup><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
fx=x3-3x2-3f"(2)2x+f"1   …i

⇒f'x=3x2-6x-32f"(2)   …ii

⇒f"x=6x-6   …iii

Then,

f"(2)=12-6=6

f"(1)=0

Now,

f'x=3x2-6x-32f"(2)

⇒f'x=3x2-6x-32×6

⇒f'(x)=3x2-6x-9

For maxima/minima, we have

f'x=0

⇒3x2-6x-9=0

⇒x2-2x-3=0

⇒x2-3x+x-3=0

⇒x=-1 and 3

Now,

f"(x)=6x-6

f"(-1)=-12<0 (maxima)

f"(3)=12>0 (minima)

Again

fx=x3-3x2-3f"(2)2x+f"1

⇒fx=x3-3x2-32×6x+0

⇒f(x)=x3-3x2-9x

⇒f(3)=27-27-9×3=-27

Let AM=x and S=MD2+MC2

So, MD2+MC2=64+x2+121+(10-x)2

⇒S=285-20x+2x2

dSdx=-20+4x⇒dSdx=0⇒x=5

d2Sdx2=4=+ve

solution:

Differentiating both side w.r.t x we get,

=-ex1-x2x2-x-1

=-ex1-x2x+1x-1

Now finding critical point by equating f'x=0

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be a polynomial of degree three that has a local maximum value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and a local minimum value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be a polynomial of degree three that has a local maximum value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and a local minimum value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>p</mi><mfenced><mn>0</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Clearly p'(x)=λ(x-1)(x-2) where λ>0

Integrating both sides

px=λx33-3x22+2x+C

Given

p1=8⇒λ13-32+2+C=8  ⇒5λ6+C=8  ...1

p(2)=4⇒λ83-6+4+C=4  ⇒23λ+C=4  ...2

By 1 and 2 ⇒C=-12

⇒p(0)=-12

q: Let f:R→R be defined as fx=-43x3+2x2+3x, x>0           3xex          ,  x≤0. Then f is increasing function in the interval
question: Let f:R→R be defined as fx=-43x3+2x2+3x, x>0           3xex          ,  x≤0. Then f is increasing function in the interval
solution:
We know that, ddxxn=nxn-1 and ddxex=ex

Hence, ddx-43x3+2x2+3x=-43×3x2+2×2x+3=-4x2+4x+3

And, using product rule, we getddx3xex=3xddxex+3exddxx=3xex+3ex=3exx+1

⇒f'x=-4x2+4x+3,x>03ex(1+x),x≤0

For x>0, f'(x)=-4x2+4x+3=-2x+12x-3

The sign scheme of f'x is

We know that, if f'x>0 then for those value of x, fx is increasing.

⇒f(x) is increasing in -12, 32.

For x≤0, f'(x)=3ex(1+x)

As ex>0, ∀ x∈R

⇒f'(x)>0 ∀ x∈(-1, 0).

So, in complete domain, f(x) is increasing in -1, 0∪-12, 32=-1, 32.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the set of all values of $p$, for which $f(x)=\left(p^2-6 p+8\right)\left(\sin ^2 2 x-\cos ^2 2 x\right)+2(2-p) x+7$ does not have any critical point, be the interval $(a, b)$. Then $16 a b$ is equal to _______
question: Let the set of all values of $p$, for which $f(x)=\left(p^2-6 p+8\right)\left(\sin ^2 2 x-\cos ^2 2 x\right)+2(2-p) x+7$ does not have any critical point, be the interval $(a, b)$. Then $16 a b$ is equal to _______
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & f(x)=-\left(p^2-6 p+8\right) \cos 4 n+2(2-p) n+7 \\ & f^1(x)=+4\left(p^2-6 p+8\right) \sin 4 x+(4-2 p) \neq 0 \\ & \sin 4 x \neq \frac{2 p-4}{4(p-4)(p-2)}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \sin 4 x \neq \frac{2(p-2)}{4(p-4)(p-2)} \\
& p \neq 2 \\
& \quad \sin 4 x \neq \frac{1}{2(p-4)} \\
& \Rightarrow\left|\frac{1}{2(p-4)}\right|>1
\end{aligned}$
on solving we get
$\therefore \mathrm{p} \in\left(\frac{7}{2}, \frac{9}{2}\right)$
Hence $\mathrm{a}=\frac{7}{2}, \mathrm{~b}=\frac{9}{2}$
$\therefore 16 \mathrm{ab}=252$

q: If the tangent to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is also tangent to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>25</mn></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to ______.
question: If the tangent to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is also tangent to the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>25</mn></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>9</mn><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
Given,

Curve y=5x2+2x-25

Now differentiating to find slope at point P2,-1 we get,

y'=10x+2 ⇒yP'=22

Now tangent to curve at P will be,

y+1=22x-2

⇒y=22x-45

Now the tangent is also tangent to the curve y=x3-x2+x and passing through a,b so plottnig the diagram we get,

Now differentiating the curve y=x3-x2+x we get,

dydx=3x2-2x+1 

dydxQ=a,b=3a2-2a+1

So, equating with tangent slope we get,

3a2-2a+1=22

⇒3a2-2a-21=0

⇒3a2-9a+7a-21=0

⇒3a+7a-3=0

Now from curve b=a3-a2+a

So, at a=3 ⇒b=21

Also a=3 & b=21 are satisfying the line y=22x-45

So, 2a+9b=2×3+9×21=195

Note at a=-7/3 tangent will be parallel so it is rejected

q:
Consider the following three statements for the function $f:(0, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f(x)=\left|\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right|-|x-1|$ :
(I) $f$ is differentiable at all $x>0$.
(II) $f$ is increasing in $(0,1)$.
(III) $f$ is decreasing in $(1, \infty)$.
Then.
question:
Consider the following three statements for the function $f:(0, \infty) \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ defined by $f(x)=\left|\log _{\mathrm{e}} x\right|-|x-1|$ :
(I) $f$ is differentiable at all $x>0$.
(II) $f$ is increasing in $(0,1)$.
(III) $f$ is decreasing in $(1, \infty)$.
Then.
solution:
For $0 < x < 1$: $f(x) = -\ln x + x - 1$, $f'(x) = -\frac{1}{x} + 1 = \frac{x-1}{x} < 0$ (decreasing).
For $x > 1$: $f(x) = \ln x - x + 1$, $f'(x) = \frac{1}{x} - 1 = \frac{1-x}{x} < 0$ (decreasing).
At $x = 1$: LHD $= \frac{1-1}{1} = 0$, RHD $= \frac{1-1}{1} = 0$. So $f$ is differentiable at $x = 1$.
(I) TRUE: $f$ is differentiable for all $x > 0$.
(II) FALSE: $f$ is decreasing in $(0, 1)$.
(III) TRUE: $f$ is decreasing in $(1, \infty)$.
Only (I) and (III) are TRUE.
